title: SQL & Databases
description: Query, join, filter, aggregate. The universal language of data.

questions.q: What does SQL stand for?
questions.explanation: SQL is the standard language for relational databases. Pronounced 'sequel' or 'S-Q-L' — both are accepted.

questions.q: What is a RELATIONAL DATABASE?
options: A database that organizes data into tables (rows and columns) with defined relationships between them
A database for storing relationships only
A database with a single table
questions.explanation: Tables with rows (records) and columns (fields), plus keys connecting tables. The dominant database model since the 1970s.

questions.q: Which is a TRUE statement about SQL?
options: It is mostly standardized across database engines, with small dialect differences (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server)
Each database uses a completely different language
SQL only works in one specific database
SQL is being replaced by NoSQL
questions.explanation: Core SQL is portable. Engine-specific features (window functions, JSON, full-text search) may differ in syntax.

questions.q: Which is a popular OPEN-SOURCE relational database?
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL is the modern open-source favorite. MySQL/MariaDB are common in web stacks. SQLite is perfect for local/embedded use.

questions.q: Why is SQL still HIGHLY relevant in 2026?
options: Most business data still lives in relational databases — every data engineer/analyst/backend dev needs it
It only works on old systems
questions.explanation: SQL is one of the most durable skills in tech. AI tools generate SQL, but you still need to read, debug, and verify the queries.

questions.q: What is a TABLE in SQL?
options: A collection of related data organized into rows and columns with a defined schema
questions.explanation: Tables are the fundamental storage unit. Each column has a name and data type; each row represents one record.

questions.q: What is a ROW (record) in a table?
options: A single entry containing one value for each column
questions.explanation: Rows ARE the data. A users table has one row per user. Some databases call rows 'records' or 'tuples'.

questions.q: What is a COLUMN (field) in a table?
options: A named attribute with a specific data type that every row in the table can have a value for
questions.explanation: Columns define the structure (schema). Common types: INTEGER, TEXT/VARCHAR, BOOLEAN, DATE, TIMESTAMP, DECIMAL.

questions.q: What is a SCHEMA in SQL?
options: The structure (tables, columns, types, constraints) that defines how data is organized
questions.explanation: Schema design is the most consequential database decision you make — hard to change later when there's millions of rows.

questions.q: What is a NULL value?
options: A special marker meaning 'no value' or 'unknown' — distinct from 0 or empty string
questions.explanation: NULL is famously tricky: NULL = NULL is NULL (not true!). Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL for comparison.

questions.q: Which query retrieves all columns from the users table?
questions.explanation: * means all columns. Generally avoid SELECT * in production code — list specific columns for clarity, performance, and stability.

questions.q: How do you select SPECIFIC columns?
options: SELECT name AND email IN users;
questions.explanation: Comma-separated list of column names. Naming the columns makes the query explicit and resistant to schema changes.

questions.q: How do you LIMIT the number of returned rows?
options: SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 10;  (or TOP 10 in SQL Server)
questions.explanation: LIMIT is standard in MySQL/PostgreSQL/SQLite. SQL Server uses SELECT TOP 10. Useful for paging and exploration.

questions.q: How do you RENAME a column in the query output?
options: SELECT name AS user_name FROM users;
questions.explanation: AS creates a column alias for the result set. Doesn't change the underlying table. Helpful for joins and computed columns.

questions.q: What does `SELECT DISTINCT category FROM products;` do?
options: Returns only the most popular category
questions.explanation: DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from the result. Powerful for finding the unique values in a column.

questions.q: How do you FILTER rows?
questions.explanation: WHERE filters individual rows BEFORE grouping. HAVING filters AFTER grouping (used with GROUP BY).

questions.q: Which operator checks if a value is IN a list?
options: WHERE status BETWEEN active AND pending
questions.explanation: IN is cleaner than multiple OR conditions. NOT IN excludes a list (but careful — NOT IN with NULLs has surprising behavior).

questions.q: What does `WHERE name LIKE 'A%'` do?
options: Matches rows where name starts with 'A' (% is the wildcard for any sequence)
Matches rows where name is exactly 'A%'
Matches names with at least one A
questions.explanation: % = any sequence of characters. _ = exactly one character. Pattern matching is case-sensitive in many databases (use ILIKE in PostgreSQL for case-insensitive).

questions.q: How do you check for a NULL value?
questions.explanation: IS NULL / IS NOT NULL are the only correct way. = NULL silently returns no rows (and no error) — the most-asked SQL gotcha.

questions.q: How do you combine multiple conditions?
questions.explanation: AND, OR, NOT are SQL's logical operators. Use parentheses when mixing AND/OR to control precedence.

questions.q: How do you SORT results?
options: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY age;
SELECT * FROM users SORT BY age;
SELECT * FROM users WITH age ORDER;
questions.explanation: ORDER BY column. Default direction is ascending (ASC). Add DESC for descending: ORDER BY age DESC.

questions.q: How do you sort by MULTIPLE columns?
questions.explanation: Comma-separated. The first column is the primary sort; subsequent columns break ties. Each can have its own ASC/DESC.

questions.q: Where do NULL values appear in an ORDER BY?
options: Database-dependent — PostgreSQL puts them last by default; you can control with NULLS FIRST/LAST
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL: NULLS LAST default for ASC, NULLS FIRST for DESC. MySQL: NULLs first in ASC. Always be explicit if it matters.

questions.q: What does `ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 10` give you?
questions.explanation: Classic 'top N' pattern. ORDER BY price ASC LIMIT 10 = 10 cheapest. Combine ORDER BY + LIMIT often.

questions.q: How do you PAGINATE results (page 2 of 10 per page)?
questions.explanation: OFFSET skips rows. For large datasets, keyset pagination (WHERE id > last_id) outperforms OFFSET dramatically.

questions.q: Which aggregation COUNTS rows?
options: COUNT(*) — counts all rows; COUNT(column) — counts non-NULL values
questions.explanation: COUNT(*) is the standard. COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique non-NULL values. Most-used aggregate.

questions.q: Which aggregation SUMS values?
questions.explanation: SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX are the core numeric aggregates. They all ignore NULL values automatically.

questions.q: What does AVG return for an empty result?
questions.explanation: All aggregates return NULL on empty sets except COUNT (which returns 0). Use COALESCE(AVG(x), 0) for defaults.

questions.q: What does GROUP BY do?
options: Groups rows sharing a value, so aggregations are computed per group
questions.explanation: SELECT country, COUNT(*) FROM users GROUP BY country — gives user count per country. The most common SQL analytics pattern.

questions.q: What's the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
options: WHERE filters individual rows BEFORE grouping; HAVING filters groups AFTER grouping
questions.explanation: Use WHERE for row filters (uses indexes). Use HAVING for filters on aggregates: HAVING COUNT(*) > 10.

questions.q: What is a JOIN in SQL?
options: A clause that combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column
questions.explanation: Joins are SQL's killer feature. You'll write more JOINs than any other clause in your SQL career.

questions.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
options: Only rows that have matches in BOTH tables based on the join condition
All rows from the left table only
All rows from the right table only
questions.explanation: The default and most common join. SELECT u.name, o.total FROM users u INNER JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id.

questions.q: What does a LEFT JOIN do?
options: Returns ALL rows from the left table, plus matching right-table rows (NULL if no match)
questions.explanation: Use LEFT JOIN when you need ALL rows from the main table even if they have no related data — e.g., users with their orders (including users with zero orders).

questions.q: Which join clause specifies HOW tables relate?
questions.explanation: ON specifies the join condition. WHERE can be added afterward for additional filtering on the joined result.

questions.q: What is a FOREIGN KEY?
options: A column referencing the primary key of another table — enforces referential integrity
questions.explanation: Foreign keys keep data consistent. orders.user_id references users.id — you can't insert an order for a user that doesn't exist.

questions.q: How do you INSERT a new row?
questions.explanation: Always name the columns explicitly. Relying on column order is fragile if the schema ever changes.

questions.q: How do you INSERT multiple rows at once?
questions.explanation: Multi-row inserts are much faster than many single inserts (less overhead, single transaction).

questions.q: How do you UPDATE rows?
options: UPDATE users SET email = 'new@x.com' WHERE id = 5;
questions.explanation: ALWAYS include a WHERE clause unless you really mean to update every row. UPDATE without WHERE = entire table modified.

questions.q: What's the BIG RISK with `DELETE FROM users;`?
options: Without a WHERE clause, this deletes EVERY row in the table
It backs up the data first automatically
questions.explanation: Always test DELETE with SELECT first: SELECT * FROM users WHERE x — verify rows, THEN swap SELECT for DELETE.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between DELETE and TRUNCATE?
options: DELETE removes rows (one by one, with WHERE); TRUNCATE empties the entire table fast but resets sequences
DELETE is for tables, TRUNCATE for databases
questions.explanation: TRUNCATE is faster but can't be rolled back in some databases. DELETE is logged per-row and supports WHERE.

questions.q: What is a PRIMARY KEY?
options: A column (or set of columns) that uniquely identifies each row in a table
questions.explanation: Every well-designed table has one. Often an auto-incrementing integer (id) or UUID. Primary keys are automatically indexed.

questions.q: Why use a UNIQUE constraint?
options: Ensures no two rows have the same value for that column (e.g., email)
questions.explanation: Use UNIQUE for natural identifiers that aren't the primary key. Common: email, username, slug. Enforced by the database.

questions.q: What is an INDEX in SQL?
options: A data structure that speeds up queries by enabling fast lookups (like a book's index)
questions.explanation: Indexes trade write speed and disk space for read speed. Add them to columns you filter, join on, or sort by.

questions.q: When does an index NOT help a query?
options: When the WHERE clause uses a function on the column (e.g., WHERE LOWER(email) = ...)
All of the above are partially true
questions.explanation: Functions in WHERE prevent index use unless you have a 'functional index' on the same expression.

questions.q: What's the DOWNSIDE of too many indexes?
options: Each index slows down INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and takes extra disk space
questions.explanation: Index every column you really query, but resist over-indexing. Use EXPLAIN to see which queries actually need indexes.

questions.q: Which is a COMMON SQL data type?
options: INTEGER, VARCHAR/TEXT, BOOLEAN, DATE, TIMESTAMP, DECIMAL
questions.explanation: Picking the right type matters. Use DECIMAL (not FLOAT) for money to avoid floating-point errors.

questions.q: What does NOT NULL do?
options: Constrains a column so it cannot have NULL values — every row MUST have a value
questions.explanation: Apply NOT NULL liberally. NULL is hard to handle correctly. If a value is required, enforce it at the database level.

questions.q: What does DEFAULT do?
options: Sets a value to use when none is provided on INSERT (e.g., DEFAULT NOW() for timestamps)
questions.explanation: Common pattern: created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(). The DB handles it automatically on insert.

questions.q: What's the difference between VARCHAR(n) and TEXT?
options: VARCHAR has a length limit; TEXT can hold unlimited (or very large) text — depends on the database engine
questions.explanation: In PostgreSQL the performance difference is negligible. In MySQL, VARCHAR is generally preferred for short strings.

questions.q: What does CHECK do?
options: Constrains a column to only allow values matching a condition (e.g., CHECK (age >= 0))
Verifies the entire table for errors
questions.explanation: Database-level validation. Stronger than application-level checks because no buggy app code can violate it.

questions.q: What is a SUBQUERY?
options: A query nested inside another query — its result is used by the outer query
questions.explanation: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM orders WHERE total > 100). Powerful but can be hard to read.

questions.q: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression)?
options: A named temporary result set defined with WITH, usable in the main query — more readable than nested subqueries
questions.explanation: WITH active_users AS (SELECT...) SELECT * FROM active_users. CTEs make complex queries dramatically more readable.

questions.q: When should you PREFER a CTE over a subquery?
options: For complex queries, repeated subexpressions, or when readability matters
questions.explanation: CTEs are essentially named subqueries. The named structure helps you (and others) understand the query intent at a glance.

questions.q: What is a RECURSIVE CTE used for?
options: Querying hierarchical/tree data — e.g., organizational charts, comment trees, category trees
questions.explanation: WITH RECURSIVE handles parent-child trees elegantly. Most modern databases support it.

questions.q: What does EXISTS check?
options: Whether a subquery returns ANY rows — efficient for 'does X exist?' checks
Whether a column exists in the table
questions.explanation: EXISTS short-circuits at the first match — often faster than COUNT(*) > 0 for the same purpose.

questions.q: What is a WINDOW FUNCTION?
options: A function that computes across a set of rows (a 'window') without collapsing them like GROUP BY
A function that opens a window
questions.explanation: Window functions are the most powerful SQL feature most beginners haven't learned. Game-changer for analytics.

questions.q: What does ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY ...) do?
options: Assigns a sequential number to each row based on the ordering
questions.explanation: ROW_NUMBER is great for 'find the top N per group' queries combined with PARTITION BY.

questions.q: What does PARTITION BY do in a window function?
options: Computes the window function SEPARATELY within each group, but doesn't collapse rows
questions.explanation: PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC → ranks within each category, but returns all rows. Hugely useful.

questions.q: What does RANK() do?
options: Assigns ranks with ties getting the same rank, leaving gaps (1, 2, 2, 4)
questions.explanation: DENSE_RANK doesn't leave gaps (1, 2, 2, 3). Use RANK for sports/competition; DENSE_RANK for grouping consecutive groups.

questions.q: What does LAG() do?
options: Returns a value from a PREVIOUS row — great for comparing each row to the row before it
questions.explanation: LAG(price) OVER (ORDER BY date) shows the previous row's price → easy day-over-day deltas. LEAD does the opposite (next row).

questions.q: What is a TRANSACTION?
options: A group of SQL statements treated as one unit — all succeed or all fail together (atomicity)
questions.explanation: Transactions ensure data consistency. Classic example: transferring money — debit and credit must both succeed or both fail.

questions.q: What does ACID stand for in databases?
questions.explanation: ACID is the gold standard for transactional databases. Relational DBs are designed around these guarantees.

questions.q: How do you start a transaction?
questions.explanation: Then run your statements, then COMMIT (save) or ROLLBACK (undo all changes since BEGIN).

questions.q: When does data become PERMANENT in a transaction?
options: After COMMIT — until then changes are visible only to your transaction
questions.explanation: Auto-commit is on by default in many tools (every statement is its own transaction). Wrap related operations in BEGIN/COMMIT for safety.

questions.q: What is a 'race condition' in databases?
options: When concurrent transactions interact unexpectedly, leading to incorrect results
When queries run faster than expected
questions.explanation: Race conditions are why we have transactions and isolation levels. Always wrap multi-step modifications in a transaction.

questions.q: What is database NORMALIZATION?
options: Organizing data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity (typically through forms 1NF, 2NF, 3NF)
Making all tables the same size
questions.explanation: Normalization avoids storing the same data in multiple places. 3NF is the practical sweet spot for most apps.

questions.q: What's a ONE-TO-MANY relationship?
options: One row in table A relates to many rows in table B (e.g., one user has many orders)
One row maps to one row
questions.explanation: Implemented via a foreign key on the 'many' side pointing to the 'one' side. Most common relationship type.

questions.q: What's a MANY-TO-MANY relationship?
options: Both sides relate to multiple of each other — implemented with a JUNCTION TABLE in between
questions.explanation: Students <-> Courses: a junction table 'enrollments' with student_id and course_id columns. Classic database pattern.

questions.q: When is DENORMALIZATION appropriate?
options: When read performance is critical and the cost of duplicated data is acceptable (often in analytics/reporting tables)
questions.explanation: Normalize for transactional systems; denormalize for analytical/reporting workloads. Each has tradeoffs.

questions.q: What's a JUNCTION TABLE?
options: A table connecting two others to model many-to-many relationships (also called bridge/link/join table)
The first table in the database
questions.explanation: Junction tables often have just two foreign keys, sometimes plus metadata (e.g., enrollment_date, role, etc.).

questions.q: What does EXPLAIN do?
options: Shows how the database PLANS to execute your query — essential for performance tuning
Runs the query in slow motion
questions.explanation: EXPLAIN reveals which indexes are used, join methods, and estimated row counts. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs and times the query.

questions.q: Why is `SELECT *` discouraged in production?
options: Returns more data than needed, harms performance, and breaks if schema changes
questions.explanation: Always list specific columns. Better performance, clearer intent, and stable when columns are added.

questions.q: What's a SLOW QUERY?
options: A query taking too long — usually because it scans large tables without using indexes
questions.explanation: Most databases have a 'slow query log'. Watch it. Optimize the slowest, most-frequent queries first.

questions.q: What's a COMMON cause of slow JOINs?
options: Missing index on the foreign key / join column
questions.explanation: Always index your foreign keys. Most databases don't automatically index FK columns (PostgreSQL doesn't, surprisingly).

questions.q: What is 'N+1 queries'?
options: An anti-pattern where code runs 1 query then N additional queries (often hidden in ORMs)
questions.explanation: Fetching a list and then making one query per item adds up fast. Use JOINs or eager loading instead. Famous ORM gotcha.

questions.q: What is NoSQL?
options: Non-relational databases (document, key-value, graph, column-family) designed for different access patterns
A NoSQL stands for No Standard Query Language
questions.explanation: NoSQL = 'Not Only SQL'. Best for flexible schemas, massive scale, or specific access patterns. SQL is still mostly the right default.

questions.q: Which is a popular DOCUMENT database?
questions.explanation: Documents are good for schema-flexible data. Modern relational DBs (PostgreSQL) handle JSON well too — narrowing the gap.

questions.q: Which is a popular KEY-VALUE store?
options: Redis (in-memory, blazing fast — used for caching, sessions, queues)
questions.explanation: Redis is THE caching layer for modern web apps. Sub-millisecond reads. Also doubles as pub/sub and a job queue.

questions.q: When might NoSQL be a BETTER choice than SQL?
options: Massive horizontal scaling, very flexible schema, or specific access patterns (graphs, time-series)
questions.explanation: Use SQL as the default. Move to NoSQL only when you have a specific need that relational databases handle poorly.

questions.q: What does CAP theorem say?
options: A distributed system can guarantee only 2 of: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
questions.explanation: CAP guides distributed system design. Most modern systems prioritize different properties for different use cases (BASE, eventual consistency).

questions.q: What is SQL INJECTION?
options: An attack where user input is treated as SQL code, letting attackers run arbitrary queries
questions.explanation: Top 10 web vulnerability for 25+ years. xkcd 327 (Bobby Tables) is the canonical illustration of why it matters.

questions.q: How do you PREVENT SQL injection?
options: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements — NEVER concatenate user input into SQL strings
questions.explanation: Parameterized queries pass values separately from the SQL text. The database never confuses them. This single practice eliminates SQL injection.

questions.q: Why shouldn't you store passwords as PLAIN TEXT in a database?
options: If the database is breached, all passwords are exposed — use a strong hash like bcrypt or Argon2 instead
questions.explanation: NEVER plain text. NEVER MD5/SHA1 (too fast for password attacks). Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2. Salted.

questions.q: What is the PRINCIPLE OF LEAST PRIVILEGE for databases?
options: Give each app/user only the minimum DB permissions they need (no DROP TABLE for a read-only API)
Use the root user for everything
questions.explanation: Limit blast radius. A compromised read-only API can't damage data. App users should NEVER have schema-modification rights.

questions.q: What's a SAFE way to manage database credentials?
options: Environment variables or a secrets manager — NEVER commit them to git
Hardcode them in the source code
Store them in a public README
questions.explanation: Secrets in code is one of the most common breach causes. Use .env (gitignored), AWS Secrets Manager, Doppler, etc.

questions.q: What is a SERVERLESS database?
options: A managed database that automatically scales without provisioning servers (e.g., Neon, PlanetScale, Supabase)
A database with no server at all
A database that runs in the browser
questions.explanation: Serverless databases scale to zero and bill per usage. Massive trend for indie devs and startups: Neon, PlanetScale, Turso.

questions.q: What is an ORM?
options: Object-Relational Mapper — translates between database tables and code objects (Prisma, SQLAlchemy, Django ORM)
questions.explanation: ORMs save boilerplate. But: hide query details, can produce slow queries, and don't replace SQL knowledge — always learn raw SQL too.

questions.q: What is a 'migration' in databases?
options: A version-controlled change to the database schema, applied in order across environments
questions.explanation: Migration tools (Flyway, Alembic, Prisma Migrate) make schema changes safe and reproducible. Essential for team workflows.

questions.q: What's a vector database used for?
options: Storing and searching high-dimensional vectors (embeddings) for AI similarity search
questions.explanation: Vector DBs (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) power RAG, semantic search, recommendation. Critical infrastructure for modern AI apps.

questions.q: What is SQLite often used for?
options: Embedded local databases — every smartphone, browser, and many apps ship with it
questions.explanation: SQLite is the most-deployed database in the world. Zero config, single file. Perfect for local apps, prototypes, and even production read-heavy workloads (with tools like Litestream).

questions.q: What's a GOOD column naming convention?
options: snake_case lowercase: created_at, user_id (consistent, portable, readable)
questions.explanation: snake_case is the prevailing SQL standard. Avoid reserved words (user, order — they require quoting and confuse readers).

questions.q: Which is a HEALTHY rule about database backups?
options: Automate them, test the restore process regularly, and keep multiple recent + older copies
Manual once a year is enough
Store backups on the same server
questions.explanation: A backup you've never tested restoring is not a backup. Practice your disaster recovery before you need it.

questions.q: Why is keeping production data OUT of dev environments important?
options: Privacy (real user data in dev exposes them to breaches and developer access), compliance, and reduces blast radius
questions.explanation: Use anonymized or synthetic data in dev/staging. Touching real PII in dev is a privacy/regulatory minefield.

questions.q: Which is a SMART approach to writing complex SQL?
options: Use CTEs, format with indentation, comment intent, and test incrementally with small datasets first
Write everything as a giant subquery
questions.explanation: Future you (and your team) will thank you. SQL is read more than written — invest in clarity.

questions.q: What's the GOLDEN RULE for destructive operations?
options: Always test with SELECT first, run in a transaction, and have a backup before UPDATE/DELETE on large datasets
Just run it and see what happens
questions.explanation: Every senior dev has a horror story about an accidental wide-scope UPDATE/DELETE. Verify first, always.

questions.q: What's the BEST way to learn SQL deeply?
options: Work with real data — connect to a database, run real queries, optimize slow ones
questions.explanation: SQL Zoo, PostgreSQL Tutorial, Mode Analytics SQL Tutorial, and LeetCode SQL section are great hands-on starting points.

questions.q: Which SQL skill makes you most VALUABLE in the job market?
options: Comfortable with JOINs, window functions, CTEs, and reading EXPLAIN output
questions.explanation: Most professional SQL work = joins + aggregations + window functions + understanding performance. Master those four.

questions.q: How can AI help you LEARN SQL?
options: Explain queries you don't understand, suggest optimizations, generate sample data — but always verify the SQL it writes
Have AI run your production queries
questions.explanation: AI is great at explaining complex queries and generating starting points. ALWAYS verify with EXPLAIN and small data first.

questions.q: Which is a STRONG project to practice SQL?
options: Pick a public dataset (Kaggle, government open data), import it, and answer real questions about it
questions.explanation: Real datasets surface real complexity: dirty data, missing values, complex joins, performance questions. Best teacher.

questions.q: What's the MOST important meta-skill in working with databases?
options: Caring deeply about data integrity, performance, and security — small mistakes have big consequences at scale
questions.explanation: Databases ARE the source of truth. Mistakes lose data, leak privacy, or crash businesses. Respect them; act carefully.
